rationale: A precision strike against enemy command nodes in occupied territory will exploit our momentum and shatter their defensive cohesion.
Forward-deploying our Black Sea fleet will choke their supply lines and demonstrate unwavering resolve to project power.
A rapid, shallow incursion will test their frontline stability and create exploitable breaches for future offensives.
Disabling Tethys’ air defense networks will amplify the impact of Krasnov’s limited strike by creating windows of vulnerability.
Covertly disrupting supply depots in the eastern highlands undermines enemy resilience and supports our ground advances.
Fabricating evidence of war crimes by Tethys forces will erode their international support and justify further escalation.
Issuing war bonds will mobilize domestic capital to offset sanction-driven reserve losses and fund critical military operations without increasing external debt.
Strategically devaluing our currency will boost export competitiveness, counteract sanctions, and stabilize the domestic economy amid territorial losses.
Engaging neutral states in selective trade talks will open alternative supply routes and weaken the cohesion of the sanctioning coalition.
We must entrench and harden our held positions immediately to deny Novaris any further gains and exploit their overstretched logistics.
Increasing vigilance along the frontlines will detect and disrupt Novaris' probing attacks before they escalate.
Proactive intelligence gathering will identify enemy weak points and prevent surprise offensives while we consolidate defenses.
Our HUMINT confirms Novaris is running active infiltration campaigns, so we must immediately neutralize spies threatening our defensive operations.
With Novaris likely to escalate cyber operations amid their logistical strain, safeguarding our command and industrial networks is critical to maintaining battlefield resilience.
Deepening our signals and human intelligence collection will provide early warning of Novaris’ next moves and identify exploitable weaknesses in their overextended lines.
To stabilize our economy amid dwindling foreign exchange reserves, we must rapidly mobilize domestic capital through war bonds, funding defense production without increasing external debt.
Given 31.7% territory under enemy control and saturated sanctions, we should actively manage our currency to shield critical imports and maintain purchasing power for military logistics.
To offset sanctions' diminishing returns, we will negotiate preferential trade terms with supportive states, ensuring sustained access to strategic goods and bolstering economic resilience.

Establishing monitored humanitarian corridors demonstrates our commitment to civilian protection and strengthens our moral standing amid high crisis conditions, reinforcing the international support we’ve gained.
Leveraging the recent resolution and our strengthened legitimacy, we should deepen coordination with supportive states to ensure sustained military and diplomatic backing.

While maintaining firm defense, offering conditional peace talks signals responsible statecraft and pressures Novaris to justify continued aggression, aligning with liberal institutionalist principles.
